name: The Lands of Sleep: A Dream Guide for Bedtime
description:
Tibetan dream yoga and bardo traditions in rhyming verse for bedtime. The ONLY grammar designed to be read AS a bedtime ritual — not just stories about sleep, but stories that accompany the actual process of falling asleep, dreaming, and waking up.

The Bardo Thodol (Tibetan Book of the Dead) describes six intermediate states between death and rebirth. This grammar reframes those bardos as six stages of a night's sleep: the threshold of falling asleep, the peaceful lands of gentle dreams, the wrathful guardians of nightmares (reframed as protectors wearing masks), the mirror of lucid dreaming, the deep of dreamless sleep, and the dawn of waking.

Reading instructions: At bedtime, read from The Threshold. For nightmare preparation, read from The Wrathful Guardians. For lucid dreaming curiosity, read from The Mirror. In the morning, read from The Dawn.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Tibetan thangka paintings from the Met and Rubin Museum (open access) — luminous peaceful and wrathful deities. Odilon Redon (1840-1916) — French Symbolist pastels, the most dream-like art in Western tradition. Paul Klee (1879-1940) — childlike geometry and color-as-mood landscapes. Hilma af Klint (1862-1944) — Swedish mystic painter, abstract spiritual visions and mandala-like compositions.

sections.Story:
Your eyes are getting HEAVY now.
Not the bad kind. Not the TIRED kind.
The kind that means a DOOR is opening
Somewhere in the back of your MIND.

Your eyelids are the CURTAINS
And behind them is a STAGE
Where tonight's dream is WAITING —
A brand new chapter, a brand new PAGE.

So let them CLOSE. Don't fight it.
Heavy eyes are just the SIGN
That the Lands of Sleep are CALLING.
Everything is fine. You're FINE.
sections.Wonder: Your eyes getting heavy is like a door opening. Not closing — OPENING. The heavier they feel, the wider the door gets. Can you feel it opening right now?
sections.Whisper: In the Bardo Thodol, the moment of death — and the moment of falling asleep — is called the chikhai bardo. It is NOT an ending but a THRESHOLD. The Tibetan masters taught that this moment contains the greatest opportunity: a flash of pure awareness (the Clear Light) appears just before consciousness dissolves. Most people miss it. The dream yoga practitioner trains to NOTICE it — even as a child, even while falling asleep.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Why, what an ignorant Tiger you are; can’t you see that I am a Man?”

“Oh, are you,” replied the Tiger, “what a piece of luck for me. I was just looking for a Man in order to kill and eat him, and you will do nicely.”

On hearing this the Woodcutter began to laugh. “Kill and eat me,” he replied; “why, don’t you know that Man is much too clever to be killed and eaten by a Tiger? Just come with me a little way and I will show you some things which only a Man knows, but which will be very useful for you to learn.”

The Tiger thought that this was a good idea, so he followed the Man through the jungle until they came to the Man’s house, which was strongly built of timber and heavy logs.

“What is that place?” said the Tiger when he saw it.

“That is called a house,” replied the Man. “I will show you how we use it.”

And so saying he went inside and shut the door.

“Now,” said he, speaking from the inside to the Tiger, “you see what a foolish creature a Tiger is compared to a Man. You poor animals live in a hole in the forest, exposed to wind, rain, cold and heat; and all your strength is of no value to make a house like this.

metadata.tibetan_source: Chikhai bardo — the bardo of the moment of death/sleep onset. From the Bardo Thodol.
metadata.archetype: The threshold crossing — the moment between one state and another (parallels every 'once upon a time,' every fairy tale's opening)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the wardrobe in Narnia — you push through the heavy coats (heavy eyelids) and suddenly you're somewhere else entirely

sections.Story:
First your TOES stop talking to you.
Then your LEGS. Then your HIPS.
Your body melts like CHOCOLATE
From your fingertips to your LIPS.

You're not LOSING your body — don't worry.
It's just going QUIET for the night.
Like a house that turns its LIGHTS off
Room by room. That's RIGHT.

First the kitchen. Then the HALLWAY.
Then the bedroom — soft and SLOW.
Your body is a house that's SLEEPING.
Let it go. Let it GO.
sections.Wonder: Can you feel your toes right now? Really feel them? Now... can you let them go quiet? Like turning off a light in a room you don't need. Your body knows how to sleep. You just have to let it.
sections.Whisper: The Bardo Thodol describes the 'dissolution of the elements' at death: earth dissolves into water (the body feels heavy), water into fire (dryness), fire into air (cooling), air into consciousness (breathing stops). The same sequence happens in miniature every night: the body progressively 'switches off' sensation from extremities inward. Yoga Nidra (yogic sleep) uses this same body-scan technique, now validated by neuroscience as effective for inducing relaxation.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Brother Tiger! Brother Tiger! Brother Tiger!”

Scarcely had the words left his lips when he heard a low, deep growl just beside him, and a great Tiger stalked out of the thicket, licking his chops.

“Here I am, Brother,” said the Tiger. “What do you want?”

“Oh! Brother Tiger,” said the Boy, “the King has said that if I do not immediately provide him with four full-grown Tigers to guard the four gates of his palace he will kill our mother and make me a slave.”

On hearing this the Tiger laughed aloud.

“Is that all?” said he. “That is easily arranged. I can get you a hundred Tigers.”

So saying, he opened his mouth, and gave forth a series of fearful roars; and in a few minutes the whole jungle seemed to be full of Tigers, hastening up from all directions. When they were all ready, the first Tiger told his brother to mount upon his back, and so, with the Boy leading the way, and the other Tigers following in procession, they all went off in a body to the King’s palace.

As they approached the palace great consternation arose; servants ran hither and thither, and the guards were called to arms.

metadata.tibetan_source: The dissolution of the five elements — a core teaching of the Bardo Thodol and foundational to dream yoga practice.
metadata.archetype: The body scan — the ancient practice of releasing the body part by part (parallels Yoga Nidra, progressive muscle relaxation, body prayer)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Olaf melting in Frozen — except instead of sad, it's PEACEFUL. Your body melts into the bed like snow melting into warm ground

sections.Story:
Close your eyes and LOOK.
Not with your eyes — they're CLOSED.
Look with the part of you that SEES
Even when the window's CLOSED.

There's a LIGHT in there. Faint.
Like a candle in another ROOM.
Not bright like the sun — more like the SUN
Remembered. A gentle, golden BLOOM.

That light is YOU. The real you.
The one that's there before the DREAM begins.
The Tibetans call it the CLEAR LIGHT.
It's the light that never DIMS.
sections.Wonder: Close your eyes right now. Can you see anything? Not with your eyes — with the INSIDE of you. There might be a faint glow, or colors, or just a feeling of light. That's the Clear Light. It's always there, even when you can't see it.
sections.Whisper: The Clear Light (ösel) is the most important concept in Tibetan dream yoga. It appears at the exact moment between waking and sleeping — a flash of pure, naked awareness before the dream-mind constructs its images. Experienced meditators can rest IN this light without falling into dream. For children, simply NOTICING it — even for a second — is the seed of all contemplative practice. The Clear Light is not something you CREATE; it is what you ARE when you stop creating.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“It is so dark in here,” replied the Prince, “that I cannot see my way at all clearly. Will you please precede me and show me the way?”

The Ogre was very angry at hearing this, but he was unable to refuse, so he started off and ran round the stove three times, the Prince following closely at his heels. But he went so fast that the Prince, although he had his knife ready in his hand, was unable to catch him; and the Ogre, seeing that the Prince was not to be outwitted by this stratagem, went upstairs to his wife, leaving the young man locked up in the kitchen, where he spent the night alone.

Next morning the Ogre started off soon after daylight on his own business, and as soon as he was gone the Prince ran upstairs to the glass room, where he found the lady lying in a trance as before. He took the flower from behind her ear, and she immediately woke up and looked about her.

“Good-morning, Prince,” said she. “How did you succeed last night? I hope you followed the instructions which I gave you.”

The Prince described to her what had occurred, and she said:

metadata.tibetan_source: Ösel (Clear Light) — the luminosity of the dharmakāya, the ground awareness that underlies all experience. Central to Dzogchen and Mahamudra.
metadata.archetype: The inner light — the divine spark within (parallels the Quaker 'Inner Light,' the Hindu Atman, the Sufi 'light upon light')
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Force in Star Wars — 'luminous beings are we, not this crude matter.' The Clear Light is what Yoda was talking about

sections.Story:
Just before you SLEEP, the shapes come.
SWIRLS and spirals. Faces made of MIST.
Colors you can't name — between purple and SOMETHING.
A whole parade that can't be MISSED.

They float across the inside of your EYES
Like clouds across a PRIVATE sky.
Don't grab them. Don't chase THEM.
Just let them float BY.

These are the PREVIEWS. The dream is LOADING.
Your mind is shuffling its CARDS.
Soon the story will BEGIN —
But for now, just watch the shifting SHARDS.
sections.Wonder: Have you ever seen swirly shapes or weird colors just before you fall asleep? That's called HYPNAGOGIA — the shapes your mind makes while it's getting ready to dream. They're like previews for tonight's movie. What shapes do YOU usually see?
sections.Whisper: Hypnagogia — the state between waking and sleep — is where the Bardo Thodol's 'appearances' manifest. The Tibetans describe the bardo as filled with spontaneous visions: lights, sounds, figures. Modern neuroscience confirms that hypnagogia involves the brain's visual cortex firing spontaneously, producing phosphenes (light patterns) and pareidolic faces. Many artists and scientists have used this state creatively — Edison, Dalí, and Einstein all practiced 'threshold thinking.' The shapes before sleep are not random noise; they are the mind's raw creative material.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Well,” replied the Musk Deer, “the facts are as follows: I was drinking just now at a stream in the forest when I noticed this Tiger shut up in a Woodcutter’s hut. The door was bolted outside, and he was unable to come out, and was in danger of starving to death, so he called out to me, asking me to release him, promising me, if I did so, that he would spare my life. I accordingly opened the door; but no sooner did the Tiger come out, than he seized upon me, saying that he was so hungry that he had really no alternative but to devour me on the spot. And when I reproached him for his bad faith, he replied that he did not know what good faith was, and, in fact, did not believe that such a thing existed. So we made a bargain that we would ask the first three living creatures we met whether or no there is such a thing as good faith in this world. If they say there is, then I am to go free; but if they say there is not, then the Tiger is at liberty to kill and eat me. We have already consulted two persons in the matter, and they are both of opinion that there is no such thing as good faith.

metadata.tibetan_source: The spontaneous appearances (nangwa) of the bardo — visions that arise naturally between states of consciousness.
metadata.archetype: The liminal vision — the images that appear at the threshold (parallels shamanic vision quests, Celtic twilight states)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the swirling colors in the Doctor Strange portal scenes — your brain is opening a portal to the dream dimension right before you fall asleep

items.name: How to Fall (It's Just Letting Go)

sections.Story:
Falling asleep is just FALLING.
Not the scary kind — the TRUST kind.
Like falling backward into someone's ARMS.
Like falling into a pillow made of WIND.

You can't MAKE yourself fall asleep.
(Have you noticed? The harder you TRY,
The more AWAKE you get.)
The secret is: stop trying. Just... LIE.

Let the bed HOLD you. Let the dark be SOFT.
Let your thoughts float away like BOATS.
You don't need to steer them ANYWHERE.
Just watch them drift. Like little paper BOATS.
sections.Wonder: Have you ever noticed that trying really hard to fall asleep keeps you AWAKE? That's because sleep isn't something you DO — it's something you LET HAPPEN. Like letting go of a balloon. You don't throw it — you just open your hand.
sections.Whisper: The Tibetan dream yoga instruction for sleep onset is remarkably similar to modern sleep hygiene: STOP TRYING. Tenzin Wangyal Rinpoche teaches that sleep comes when you 'release grasping' — the more you clutch at sleep, the more it eludes you. This mirrors the Buddhist teaching on attachment: the tighter you hold, the more you lose. Sleep is the nightly lesson in non-attachment. Every human learns this teaching every night, whether they know it or not.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Yes, please, Brother Hare,” said the Tiger, “I think a fire would be very pleasant.”

So the Hare lighted a fire just in front of the Tiger, and when it was blazing up he kept putting the sticks nearer and nearer the Tiger, so that the Tiger was obliged to keep edging further and further away, when all of a sudden he toppled over backwards into the gulf behind. Now it happened that half-way down the gulf a tree was growing from a cleft in the precipice, and as he passed this the Tiger seized one of the boughs with his teeth, and so arrested his fall. The Hare, peeping over the edge, saw what had happened, and he called out:

“Oh, Uncle Tiger, Uncle Tiger, are you safe?”

The Tiger was afraid to open his mouth to reply, and all he could do was to growl, “M—m—m——”

“Oh, Uncle Tiger,” said the Hare, “is that all you can say? I am afraid you must be very badly hurt.

metadata.tibetan_source: The practice of 'releasing grasping' — from Tenzin Wangyal's dream yoga teachings and the general Dzogchen instruction on non-fabrication.
metadata.archetype: The surrender — the moment of releasing control (parallels the Tao's wu wei, the Christian 'let go and let God,' the trust fall)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Buzz Lightyear learning to fly: 'That's not flying — that's falling with STYLE.' Sleep isn't something you achieve; it's something you fall into, beautifully

sections.Story:
There's a BRIDGE between awake and asleep.
You can't see it, but you BREATHE it.
In... and out. In... and OUT.
Each breath a plank. You weave it.

Breathe IN: you're still a little bit AWAKE.
Breathe OUT: you're a little more ASLEEP.
In: awake. Out: DREAMING.
In: the shore. Out: the DEEP.

Don't count your breaths — just FEEL them.
Each one carries you a little FURTHER.
The bridge is made of AIR and trust
And the gentle rhythm of your MURMUR.
sections.Wonder: Your breath is a bridge between being awake and being asleep. Each breath-out carries you a little further across. Can you feel yourself crossing? Breathe in — you're still here. Breathe out — you're a little bit in the dream world...
sections.Whisper: Prāṇāyāma (breath awareness) is the most universal contemplative technique on Earth — found in every tradition from Buddhist ānāpānasati to Christian hesychasm to Sufi dhikr. In Tibetan dream yoga, the breath is specifically called the 'vehicle of the mind' (rlung gi rta). Where the breath goes, the mind follows. Slow, rhythmic breathing literally changes brainwave patterns from beta (alert) to alpha (relaxed) to theta (pre-sleep). The bridge is neurologically real.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

As he stood upon the beach watching the young birds they suddenly began to manifest every sign of terror and confusion, chattering and squealing wildly to one another, and flapping their puny wings; and on turning towards the lake in order to ascertain what was the cause of their alarm, he perceived an immense Dragon—whose head, at the end of its long neck, towered high above the water—making its way rapidly across the lake, with the evident intention of devouring the young Gryphons. The young Man, who was of a courageous and kindly disposition, determined to save the young Gryphons from the maw of this monster; so, drawing his sword, he waited till the Dragon had set foot upon dry land, and then, attacking him fiercely, he engaged single-handed in a desperate conflict.

metadata.tibetan_source: Rlung (wind/breath) practice — from the Six Yogas of Naropa and dream yoga preliminary practices.
metadata.archetype: The breath as vehicle — the universal technique of using breath to cross between states (parallels every meditation tradition on Earth)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Mr. Miyagi's 'breathe in, breathe out' — the simplest instruction that turns out to be the most powerful technique. Wax on, wax off, breathe in, breathe out

sections.Story:
There's a GATE. You've passed through it
Every single night since you were BORN.
You don't remember because the GATE
Is made of forgetting — soft and WORN.

It doesn't open OUTWARD like a door.
It opens INWARD — into you.
The deeper in you GO, the wider it gets.
The whole dream world is INSIDE you. Who knew?

So go IN. Go deeper. Go FURTHER.
Past your thoughts, past your NAME,
Past everything you think you ARE.
The dream is waiting. It knows you CAME.
sections.Wonder: Every night you pass through an invisible gate into the dream world. You've done it thousands of times! But the gate doesn't open outward — it opens INWARD. The dream world is inside you. Close your eyes and go in...
sections.Whisper: The 'gate that opens inward' is a core insight of both Tibetan Buddhism and depth psychology: the unconscious is not 'out there' — it is deeper IN. Jung called this the process of individuation; the Tibetans call it the recognition of mind's nature (semnyi ngowo). The dream world is not a separate place you TRAVEL to; it is a dimension of your own mind that you normally don't access while awake. Sleep doesn't take you somewhere else — it takes you deeper into where you already are.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

On hearing this Baki at once promised to follow the lady’s directions, and she released him from the cavern. Going round to the back of the rock, he found himself face to face with another great rock standing by itself. Kicking this rock three times with his right foot, he pronounced the magic words, and as he said them for the third time two rocky doors flew open, disclosing a cave inside. Entering the cave he saw a green Parrot seated on a red stone in the centre, and he at once seized the bird and wrung its neck. As soon as he had accomplished this he ran hastily back to the main cavern, and as he approached the entrance he saw the Ogre, who had just been returning to his home, lying across the threshold stone dead, with his neck all twisted.

metadata.tibetan_source: The bardo entrance — the transition point between waking and dream consciousness. Related to the Dzogchen teaching on 'the door of the mind.'
metadata.archetype: The inner journey — the discovery that the quest leads inward (parallels Alice's rabbit hole, the wardrobe to Narnia, the looking glass)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the scene in The Matrix where Neo is offered the red pill — except every night, your brain offers you the pill automatically. You've been taking it since birth

items.name: The Dream Where You Can Fly

sections.Story:
Tonight you might FLY.
Not in a plane — just YOU, arms wide,
Lifting off the ground like a THOUGHT
That decided to take the sky for a RIDE.

The houses get SMALL. The trees are DOTS.
The whole world becomes a MAP below.
And you think: 'I'm FLYING. I'm actually FLYING.'
And the wind says: 'I KNOW.'

Don't flap your arms — that's not HOW.
Flying in dreams is done with your HEART.
You just BELIEVE you can go up,
And up you go. That's the art.
sections.Wonder: Flying dreams are the most common wonderful dream in the world. Almost everyone has them! In dreams, you fly not with wings but with BELIEF. If you could fly anywhere tonight, where would you go? What would the world look like from up there?
sections.Whisper: Flying dreams occur across every culture and throughout history — they are among the most universal human experiences. In Tibetan dream yoga, flying in a dream is considered a sign that the dreamer is approaching lucidity: the recognition that the laws of physics don't apply means the dream-mind is becoming aware of its own nature. Flying is the dream's way of TELLING you it's a dream. Neuroscience suggests flying dreams may correlate with the vestibular system's disengagement during REM sleep — the inner ear stops tracking gravity, and the brain interprets this as weightlessness.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

When he arrived at the house the parents of his Bride hurried out to greet him, and after asking him why he was so late, they led him to the room where the wedding feast was laid out. All the friends and neighbours from round about were gathered there ready to share in the feast, and to offer their congratulations to the Bride and Bridegroom.

During the progress of the feast the young Mussulman, who was of a very kindly disposition, and very fond of his Mother, kept thinking to himself how he could save something nice for her to eat from amongst so much plenty. So he picked from the table a narrow-mouthed copper vessel and concealed it in his lap, and whilst eating his food he every now and then dropped into it some particularly succulent dainty, which he thought his Mother would enjoy. Presently, however, he inadvertently thrust his hand right into the vessel, and to his horror he found that he was unable to withdraw it again. In this awkward predicament he was unable to eat anything, and the Bride’s parents noticing that he no longer partook of any food, kept pressing him to have a little more.

metadata.tibetan_source: The peaceful visions of the chönyid bardo — luminous, spacious, liberated appearances. Flying as a dream yoga sign of progress.
metadata.archetype: The flight dream — the universal human dream of transcending gravity (parallels Icarus, Peter Pan, every superhero's first flight)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the moment in every superhero movie where they FLY for the first time — Miles Morales leaping off the building, Superman's first flight, Iron Man's first suit-up. That feeling? You get it for FREE in dreams

items.name: The Garden That Knows Your Name

sections.Story:
Sometimes you'll dream of a GARDEN.
Not a regular garden — a PERFECT one.
Flowers you've never seen AWAKE,
Colors that only exist under dream SUN.

The trees know your NAME here.
The grass bends toward you as you WALK.
The flowers open when you're NEAR
And the butterflies almost TALK.

This garden has always been HERE.
It was here before you were BORN.
It'll be here when you're very, very OLD.
It's YOUR garden. Never torn.
sections.Wonder: Many people dream of a beautiful garden that feels like HOME — even though they've never been there awake. Some people call it their 'soul garden.' If you have a dream garden, what grows there? What does it smell like? Who else is there?
sections.Whisper: The 'pure land' (dag zhing) in Tibetan Buddhism is a realm created by a buddha's compassion — a perfect environment for awakening. The most famous is Sukhāvatī (Dewachen), Amitābha's Western Pure Land. But in dream yoga, the pure land is understood as a quality of MIND, not a geographical place. When the mind is at peace, it spontaneously generates beauty. The garden dream is your mind showing you what it looks like when it's not worried. The garden IS your mind, resting.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“This grain is a present in return for your kindness to me. Plant it in your garden and see what grows up.”

The rich man was greatly delighted on hearing this, and thought to himself that he would soon be the possessor of beautiful jewels like his Neighbour. He prepared a bed very carefully in his garden, and planted the grain in the richest part of the soil. Every day he used to go and watch the spot, carefully examining the young shoots to see how they were getting on.

The seeds sprouted and grew very fast, and one morning, when he went out as usual to see how his crop was doing, to his astonishment he found that instead of a few stalks of barley, as he had expected, a great fierce-looking man, with a bundle of papers under his arm, was standing in the middle of the bed. The rich man was very frightened at seeing this truculent-looking stranger, and asked who he was.

“I was a creditor of yours in one of your former existences,” replied the Apparition.

metadata.tibetan_source: The pure lands (dag zhing) — buddha-fields of perfect conditions. Sukhāvatī (Dewachen) in the Amitābha tradition.
metadata.archetype: The paradise garden — the enclosed sacred space (parallels Eden, the Persian pairidaeza, the Polynesian Hawaiki, the Islamic jannah)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Secret Garden — a hidden, magical place that only you can find, where everything grows perfectly and the world is exactly right

items.name: The Animals Who Talk in Dreams

sections.Story:
In dreams, ANIMALS talk.
Not in words — in KNOWING.
A fox looks at you and you UNDERSTAND
What it's saying without its lips MOVING.

A bear might sit beside you, warm and HUGE,
And you just KNOW: 'You're safe with me.'
A bird might land upon your SHOULDER
And sing you what you need to SEE.

Dream animals are MESSENGERS.
They come from the deep part of your MIND.
The part that knows things your WAKING brain forgot.
Listen. They're KIND.
sections.Wonder: Have you ever dreamed about an animal that seemed to be trying to TELL you something? Dream animals are like messengers from the wise part of your brain. What animal visits YOUR dreams most often? What do you think it's saying?
sections.Whisper: In Tibetan tradition, animal appearances in dreams are often considered manifestations of dharma protectors (dharmapāla) or wisdom dakinis in animal form. The snow lion, garuda, tiger, and dragon are the four dignities of Shambhala — each representing a quality of awakened mind. Jung independently identified 'animal guides' as expressions of the instinctual wisdom of the unconscious — the part of the psyche that is older and wiser than the rational mind. Dream animals speak in the language of FEELING, not words.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Brother Tiger! Brother Tiger! Brother Tiger!”

Scarcely had the words left his lips when he heard a low, deep growl just beside him, and a great Tiger stalked out of the thicket, licking his chops.

“Here I am, Brother,” said the Tiger. “What do you want?”

“Oh! Brother Tiger,” said the Boy, “the King has said that if I do not immediately provide him with four full-grown Tigers to guard the four gates of his palace he will kill our mother and make me a slave.”

On hearing this the Tiger laughed aloud.

“Is that all?” said he. “That is easily arranged. I can get you a hundred Tigers.”

So saying, he opened his mouth, and gave forth a series of fearful roars; and in a few minutes the whole jungle seemed to be full of Tigers, hastening up from all directions. When they were all ready, the first Tiger told his brother to mount upon his back, and so, with the Boy leading the way, and the other Tigers following in procession, they all went off in a body to the King’s palace.

As they approached the palace great consternation arose; servants ran hither and thither, and the guards were called to arms.

metadata.tibetan_source: The four dignities of Shambhala (snow lion, garuda, tiger, dragon) — Chögyam Trungpa's presentation of Tibetan warrior archetypes.
metadata.archetype: The animal guide — the wise creature who leads the hero (parallels the Polynesian shark-guide, the Egyptian animal gods, shamanic power animals)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Hedwig in Harry Potter or Appa in Avatar — the animal companion who seems to understand you better than any human does

sections.Story:
Below every dream there's an OCEAN.
You might not always see IT,
But you can feel it — deep and DARK
And warm. You can almost BREATHE in it.

Sometimes the dream takes you DOWN there.
You sink through layers of BLUE,
Past fish that glow, past coral that HUMS,
Into water that somehow knows YOU.

Don't be afraid of the DEEP.
The ocean under the dream is WHERE
All the dreams come FROM — and go back TO.
It's the biggest, softest PRAYER.
sections.Wonder: Under every dream, there's an ocean — deep and warm and glowing. Have you ever had a water dream? Dreams about water usually mean your feelings are very DEEP right now. What's in YOUR ocean tonight?
sections.Whisper: The 'primordial ocean' appears in the dreams of every culture — Jung called it the collective unconscious, the shared depth beneath all individual minds. In Tibetan cosmology, the nagas (serpent spirits) dwell in underwater palaces beneath the earth, guarding treasures and terma (hidden teachings). Water in dreams is universally associated with EMOTION and the unconscious. The deeper the water, the deeper the feeling. The ocean under the dream is the mind's way of showing you your own depth.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

This reply made the Ogre very angry, but he was unable to refuse to do as the Prince had asked him. So the Prince took his seat on the Ogre’s chair and the Ogre kneeling on the ground before him proceeded to kow-tow three times in the orthodox manner. As the Ogre’s face touched the ground the first time the Prince drew his sword; as it touched the ground the second time he raised the sword above his head; and as it touched the ground the third and last time the Prince delivered a violent blow, completely severing the Ogre’s head from his body. Leaving the body where it lay, the Prince ran up to the glass room as fast as he could, and having awakened the lady from her sleep, he told her what had happened.

“Well done!” said she. “The first part of your task is now accomplished; but as I told you before, it is still necessary to destroy the Ogre’s mascot, or he will come to life again in a short time. What you must do now, therefore, is as follows: you must descend into the vaults below the castle, and having traversed nine dark subterranean chambers, you will come to a blank stone wall.

metadata.tibetan_source: Naga realms beneath the waters — repositories of hidden teachings (terma). The primordial ocean also parallels Egyptian Nun.
metadata.archetype: The primordial waters — the deep unconscious beneath all consciousness (parallels Nun, Polynesian Moana, the Jungian collective unconscious)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the ocean in Moana — it's not just water, it's ALIVE, it has a personality, and it's trying to show you something. The dream ocean is YOUR ocean

items.name: The People Who Love You from Far Away

sections.Story:
Sometimes in dreams you'll see someone
You've LOST. Someone who went AWAY.
A grandparent. A pet. A FRIEND.
Someone who couldn't STAY.

And in the dream, they're RIGHT THERE.
Real as anything. WARM.
They might hug you. They might SMILE.
They might just stand and keep you from HARM.

These are not 'just' DREAMS.
In the Tibetan TRADITION,
The ones who love you visit when you SLEEP.
This is their MISSION.
sections.Wonder: Have you ever dreamed about someone you miss — someone who moved away or died? In Tibet, they believe those dreams are REAL visits. The people who love you can reach you in your sleep. Who do you hope visits your dreams tonight?
sections.Whisper: The Bardo Thodol describes the deceased appearing to the living in dreams — not as memory, but as actual CONTACT across the bardo. In Tibetan culture, dreams of the dead are taken very seriously: monasteries perform specific rituals when a student reports a visitation dream. Whether one interprets this literally (the dead visiting) or psychologically (the unconscious processing grief), these dreams provide genuine comfort and connection. Research by dream scientists like Deirdre Barrett confirms that 'visitation dreams' have a qualitatively different feel from ordinary dreams — they feel MORE real, not less.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

FOLK TALES FROM TIBET

WITH ILLUSTRATIONS BY A TIBETAN ARTIST AND SOME VERSES FROM TIBETAN LOVE-SONGS

COLLECTED AND TRANSLATED BY CAPT. W. F. O’CONNOR, C.I.E. Secretary and Interpreter of the Mission to Lhasa (1904)

LONDON HURST AND BLACKETT, LTD. 182, HIGH HOLBORN, W.C.

1906

All rights reserved

PREFACE.

In presenting these little stories to the public, it may perhaps be of interest if I describe how I came by them.

During two years spent in Tibet, at Gyantse, Lhasa, and elsewhere, I have made many friends amongst all classes of Tibetans—high and low, rich and poor—and have conversed with all sorts of persons upon all sorts of topics. In the course of my wanderings I learned that there exists amongst this fascinating and little-known people a wealth of folk-lore, hitherto inaccessible to the outside world, and I made efforts to collect as many of their stories as I could.

metadata.tibetan_source: The bardo of becoming (sipa bardo) — where the consciousness of the deceased encounters the living in dreams. Also the practice of phowa (consciousness transference).
metadata.archetype: The dream of the dead — the beloved returning in sleep (parallels Egyptian Ra visiting the dead, Japanese Obon, Day of the Dead, Polynesian ancestor visits)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Mufasa appearing in the clouds to Simba — 'Remember who you are.' Except in dreams, it's not a cloud. It feels completely, totally REAL

items.name: The Colors That Don't Exist When You're Awake

sections.Story:
In dreams, there are COLORS
That don't exist when you're AWAKE.
A blue that's also somehow MUSIC.
A gold that tastes like CAKE.

The Tibetans say five COLORS
Hold the secret of the MIND:
White like space. Blue like MIRROR.
Yellow like richness. Red like KIND.

Green like everything that GROWS.
Five wisdom colors, BRIGHT and true.
When you see them in a DREAM,
That's the wisest part of YOU.
sections.Wonder: Have you ever seen a color in a dream that you couldn't find when you woke up? Dream colors can be MORE than regular colors — they can FEEL like things, or sound like things. What's the most amazing color you've ever dreamed?
sections.Whisper: The five wisdom colors (white, blue, yellow, red, green) correspond to the five dhyāni buddhas and the five wisdoms: mirror-like wisdom (blue/Akshobhya), wisdom of equanimity (yellow/Ratnasambhava), discriminating wisdom (red/Amitābha), all-accomplishing wisdom (green/Amoghasiddhi), and wisdom of dharmadhātu (white/Vairochana). In the Bardo Thodol, these colors appear as brilliant lights — the brighter and more vivid the color in your dream, the closer you are to pure awareness. Synesthesia (colors that sound or taste) in dreams may reflect the brain's cross-wiring of sensory systems during REM sleep.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“This grain is a present in return for your kindness to me. Plant it in your garden and see what grows up.”

The rich man was greatly delighted on hearing this, and thought to himself that he would soon be the possessor of beautiful jewels like his Neighbour. He prepared a bed very carefully in his garden, and planted the grain in the richest part of the soil. Every day he used to go and watch the spot, carefully examining the young shoots to see how they were getting on.

The seeds sprouted and grew very fast, and one morning, when he went out as usual to see how his crop was doing, to his astonishment he found that instead of a few stalks of barley, as he had expected, a great fierce-looking man, with a bundle of papers under his arm, was standing in the middle of the bed. The rich man was very frightened at seeing this truculent-looking stranger, and asked who he was.

“I was a creditor of yours in one of your former existences,” replied the Apparition.

metadata.tibetan_source: The five wisdom colors of the dhyāni buddhas — from the Bardo Thodol's description of the peaceful deities' appearances.
metadata.archetype: The impossible color — the beauty that exists only in altered states (parallels the color out of space, the music of the spheres, the beatific vision)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the colors in the 'trip sequence' of 2001: A Space Odyssey or the soul world in Pixar's Soul — colors that feel like MORE than colors, colors that feel like meaning itself

items.name: The Dream Where Something Chases You

sections.Story:
Here's a dream you KNOW:
Something's behind you. RUNNING.
You can hear its FEET — or is it paws? —
And your heart is drumming, DRUMMING.

You run through HALLWAYS. Through doors that SHRINK.
Your legs feel like they're made of GLUE.
The thing gets CLOSER. Closer. CLOSER.
And you think: 'It's coming for ME. What do I DO?'

Here's the SECRET the Tibetans knew:
The thing that CHASES you in dreams?
It's not trying to HURT you.
It's trying to REACH you. Nothing's what it SEEMS.
sections.Wonder: Almost everyone has chase dreams! Here's the secret: the thing chasing you usually isn't trying to hurt you — it's trying to REACH you. It might be a feeling you're running from, or a truth you're not ready to hear. What if, next time, you stopped running and turned around?
sections.Whisper: The wrathful deities (trowo) in the Bardo Thodol are IDENTICAL to the peaceful deities — same beings, different appearance. They look terrifying because the dreamer's own fear distorts them. The instruction is always: RECOGNIZE them. 'Whatever appears, know it as your own mind's projection.' Chase dreams are the most common nightmare worldwide. Gestalt dream therapy (Fritz Perls) mirrors the Tibetan approach: every element of the dream IS the dreamer. The monster chasing you is a part of yourself you've disowned, trying to reunite.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

Once upon a time there was a Cat who lived in a large farm-house in which there was a great number of Mice. For many years the Cat found no difficulty in catching as many Mice as she wanted to eat, and she lived a very peaceful and pleasant life. But as time passed on she found that she was growing old and infirm, and that it was becoming more and more difficult for her to catch the same number of Mice as before; so after thinking very carefully what was the best thing to do, she one day called all the Mice together, and after promising not to touch them, she addressed them as follows:

“Oh! Mice,” said she, “I have called you together in order to say something to you. The fact is that I have led a very wicked life, and now, in my old age, I repent of having caused you all so much inconvenience and annoyance. So I am going for the future to turn over a new leaf. It is my intention now to give myself up entirely to religious contemplation and no longer to molest you, so henceforth you are at liberty to run about as freely as you will without fear of me.

metadata.tibetan_source: The wrathful deities (trowo) of the chönyid bardo — terrifying appearances that are actually the peaceful deities in wrathful form.
metadata.archetype: The chase dream — the universal nightmare of being pursued (parallels every mythology's monsters, the Furies, the Wild Hunt)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the Dementors in Harry Potter — they seem terrifying, but the REAL fear is what they represent (your worst memories). Face them with a Patronus (a happy memory) and they lose their power

sections.Story:
FALLING. The ground disappears.
The floor opens up and you DROP.
Down and down and down and DOWN
And you can't. Make it. STOP.

Your stomach flips. The wind ROARS.
Everything rushes UP past your face.
And you think: 'If I HIT the bottom—'
But here's the secret about this PLACE:

There IS no bottom. You're not really FALLING.
You're FLYING — just pointed the wrong way DOWN.
Turn around. Look UP. Spread your arms.
The falling dream? It's the flying dream — turned AROUND.
sections.Wonder: Falling dreams feel so scary — but here's an amazing secret. A falling dream and a flying dream are the SAME dream, just in different directions! Next time you're falling in a dream, try turning around. You might start flying instead. What direction would you go?
sections.Whisper: The 'falling asleep jerk' (hypnic jerk) is a myoclonic twitch that occurs as the brain transitions from waking to sleep — the body's muscles contract suddenly, often producing a vivid sensation of falling. The brain misinterprets the muscle relaxation as freefall. In dream yoga, falling is understood as the mind encountering GROUNDLESSNESS (shunyata) — the absence of solid reference points. Instead of being terrified by the lack of ground, the practitioner is taught to REST in the openness. Falling IS freedom, once the fear dissolves.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Yes, please, Brother Hare,” said the Man, thinking he would sell the skin and make a lot of money.

So the Hare pointed out to him where the dead Tiger lay in the ravine, and the Man hastened off to skin it, after first asking the Hare to take care of his Horses while he was away.

As soon as he was out of sight the Hare saw two Ravens sitting in a tree overhead. He called out to them:

“Brothers Raven, look here! Here are a lot of Horses with no one in charge. Why don’t you come down and feed on the sores on their backs?”

The Ravens thought this was a good idea, and flying down, they perched on the Horses’ backs, and began to dig their beaks into the sore places. The poor Horses, in fear and pain, soon stampeded, and galloped about all over the country.

The Hare then hopped on a little further down the road and came upon a Boy tending Sheep.

“Good-morning, Brother Boy,” said the Hare, “would you like to know where there is a fine Raven’s nest, full of eggs?”

“Yes, please, Brother Hare,” said the Boy, thinking he would climb the tree and take the Raven’s eggs.

metadata.tibetan_source: The experience of groundlessness in the bardo — the dissolution of reference points that can terrify or liberate depending on recognition.
metadata.archetype: The fall — the universal dream of losing ground (parallels the Fall in Genesis, Icarus, the Tower card in Tarot)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Miles Morales in Spider-Verse — he's falling, falling, and then he realizes: 'I'm not falling. I'm RISING.' The same physics, the same speed, but the MEANING flips

items.name: The Dream Where You Can't Find Home

sections.Story:
You're LOST. The streets are wrong.
The houses look FAMILIAR but they're NOT.
Your house should be RIGHT HERE —
But it's gone. An empty LOT.

You walk and walk. Turn LEFT, turn RIGHT.
Every corner leads to somewhere STRANGE.
People pass but no one SEES you.
The world has shifted out of RANGE.

But here's what the DREAM is saying:
You're not lost at all — you're LOOKING.
Looking for something you haven't FOUND yet.
The dream is a kitchen. Something's COOKING.
sections.Wonder: Dreams where you're lost and can't find home are really common. But what if 'lost' is just another word for 'searching'? You're not lost — you're on a quest! You just haven't found what you're looking for YET. What do you think the dream wants you to find?
sections.Whisper: The bardo is literally described as a state of WANDERING (bar do = 'between two'). The consciousness wanders between lives, between dreams, between states. Being 'lost' in the bardo is not a malfunction — it IS the bardo. The Bardo Thodol's entire purpose is to provide ORIENTATION in this wandering state. 'Lost' dreams often correspond to life transitions: moving, changing schools, losing a friendship. The dream-mind processes geographical dislocation as a map search. You ARE searching — for a new sense of home.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

The two animals now entered into conversation with one another, and soon striking up quite a friendship, the Monkey led away the Tortoise into the jungle, and showed him a comfortable cave where he could spend the night. The Tortoise was so interested with all he saw and so pleased with his friend the Monkey, that he remained for several days in the forest, moving about during the day and sleeping with the Monkey in the cave every night.

Meanwhile Mrs. Tortoise was becoming rather anxious concerning her husband’s prolonged absence. He had never been away from home for so long before, so finally she despatched one of the young Tortoises to find out where his father was and how he was getting on. The young Tortoise accordingly swam to land, and after hunting about for some time in the forest he came across his father near the cave.

“Good-morning, Father,” said he, “Mother has sent me to find out where you are and how you are getting on.”

“Oh, I am all right, my boy,” replied Father Tortoise; “tell Mother she need not trouble about me. My friend, Brother Monkey, and I are just having a good time in the forest, and I will be home in a few days.

metadata.tibetan_source: The bardo of becoming (sipa bardo) — the wandering state between death and rebirth, characterized by confusion and searching.
metadata.archetype: The lost wanderer — the hero who must find their way home (parallels Odysseus, the Israelites in the desert, Dorothy in Oz)
metadata.pop_culture: Like when Coraline goes through the little door and finds the Other World — everything LOOKS like home but ISN'T. The dream is telling you: the home you're looking for might be different from the one you left

items.name: The Monster with Too Many Teeth

sections.Story:
There's a MONSTER in your dream.
It has too many TEETH.
Its eyes are too BIG. Its claws are too SHARP.
It breathes fire or frost or something UNDERNEATH.

You want to SCREAM. You want to RUN.
Your body freezes like a ROCK.
But the Tibetans say: 'LOOK at it.
Look at the monster. Don't TALK.

Just LOOK.' Because monsters in dreams
Are wearing COSTUMES. Underneath
The terrible mask is something KIND.
The monster is a GUARDIAN. Not a thief.
sections.Wonder: The Tibetans say that every scary thing in a dream is actually a GUARDIAN wearing a scary mask. If you could see behind the monster's mask, you'd see something kind. Next time a monster appears in your dream, try looking RIGHT AT IT. What might be behind the mask?
sections.Whisper: The wrathful deities of the Bardo Thodol are depicted with skull crowns, flaming aureoles, fangs, and multiple arms holding weapons — they are DELIBERATELY terrifying. But the text explicitly states: these are the SAME BEINGS as the peaceful deities, appearing in wrathful form to break through the ego's defenses. The instruction is to recognize them as 'your own mind's radiance' and embrace them. This is radical: the monster is not OTHER. It is a part of you, dressed up in fear. Modern psychology confirms: facing dream figures reduces their power. Avoidance amplifies fear.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

As he stood upon the beach watching the young birds they suddenly began to manifest every sign of terror and confusion, chattering and squealing wildly to one another, and flapping their puny wings; and on turning towards the lake in order to ascertain what was the cause of their alarm, he perceived an immense Dragon—whose head, at the end of its long neck, towered high above the water—making its way rapidly across the lake, with the evident intention of devouring the young Gryphons. The young Man, who was of a courageous and kindly disposition, determined to save the young Gryphons from the maw of this monster; so, drawing his sword, he waited till the Dragon had set foot upon dry land, and then, attacking him fiercely, he engaged single-handed in a desperate conflict.

metadata.tibetan_source: The 58 wrathful deities of the chönyid bardo — herukas and dakinis with terrifying forms that are actually liberating.
metadata.archetype: The guardian monster — the terrifying being that is actually a protector (parallels gargoyles, the Sphinx, temple guardian lions)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Sulley in Monsters Inc. — literally a monster whose JOB is to scare you, but who turns out to be the kindest character in the movie. The monster is your friend

sections.Story:
Sometimes the dream goes DARK.
Not dark like nighttime — DARKER.
Dark like a room with no WINDOWS.
Dark like the inside of a MARKER.

You can't see ANYTHING. Not your hands,
Not the walls, not the FLOOR.
Just dark. And in the dark, you FEEL
Something. Just behind a DOOR.

But dark in dreams is not DANGEROUS.
Dark is just a room that hasn't found its LIGHT.
YOU are the light. You've always BEEN.
Close your dream-eyes. Open your SIGHT.
sections.Wonder: Dreams that are very, very dark can feel scary. But here's a secret: in dreams, YOU are the light source. You don't need a flashlight or a lamp. You just need to remember that you GLOW. Can you imagine yourself glowing in the dark? What color would your light be?
sections.Whisper: Darkness in the Bardo Thodol is not absence — it is the dharmakāya, the 'truth body,' the ultimate nature of reality BEFORE it takes form. The darkness is not empty; it is FULL of potential. The terror comes from the mind's habit of needing something to SEE. When there is nothing to see, the mind panics. The dream yoga instruction is: rest in the darkness without needing to see. The darkness itself is luminous — 'self-luminous emptiness' (rang bzhin ösel stong pa nyid). You are the light you're looking for.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“Oh! Mice,” said she, “I have called you together in order to say something to you. The fact is that I have led a very wicked life, and now, in my old age, I repent of having caused you all so much inconvenience and annoyance. So I am going for the future to turn over a new leaf. It is my intention now to give myself up entirely to religious contemplation and no longer to molest you, so henceforth you are at liberty to run about as freely as you will without fear of me. All I ask of you is that twice every day you should all file past me in procession and each one make an obeisance as you pass me by, as a token of your gratitude to me for my kindness.”

When the Mice heard this they were greatly pleased, for they thought that now, at last, they would be free from all danger from their former enemy, the Cat. So they very thankfully promised to fulfil the Cat’s conditions, and agreed that they would file past her and make a salaam twice every day.

So when evening came the Cat took her seat on a cushion at one end of the room, and the Mice all went by in single file, each one making a profound salaam as it passed.

metadata.tibetan_source: The darkness of the bardo — the dharmakāya luminosity that appears as terrifying darkness to the unprepared mind.
metadata.archetype: The dark night — the encounter with absolute darkness that precedes illumination (parallels the Dark Night of the Soul, Jonas in the whale, Ra in the Duat)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the scene in Harry Potter when Dumbledore says, 'Happiness can be found, even in the darkest of times, if one only remembers to turn on the light.' In dreams, YOU are the light

items.name: What Happens When You Turn Around

sections.Story:
Here's the bravest thing you'll ever DO in a dream:
Turn AROUND.
When something chases you — TURN.
When something scares you — STAND your ground.

Because nightmares are SHY.
They need you to RUN to stay alive.
The moment you FACE them? They shrink.
They stutter. They barely SURVIVE.

The monster becomes a KITTEN.
The cliff becomes a STEP.
The darkness becomes a BLANKET.
Every nightmare is a promise KEPT — by your own mind, trying to make you BRAVE.
sections.Wonder: Nightmares need you to run away — that's what gives them power. The moment you stop and turn around, the nightmare usually transforms into something much smaller and gentler. Have you ever been brave enough to face something scary? What happened?
sections.Whisper: This is the central instruction of the entire Bardo Thodol, distilled to its essence: TURN TOWARD what frightens you. In Tibetan, this is called 'recognition' (ngo shes) — the moment you recognize that the terrifying appearance is your own mind's projection, it transforms. This is not positive thinking or denial. It is the deepest form of courage: looking directly at what you fear. Clinical research on nightmare treatment confirms this: the most effective technique for recurring nightmares is 'imagery rehearsal therapy,' in which the dreamer practices FACING the nightmare figure while awake. The Tibetans discovered this 1,200 years ago.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

As he stood upon the beach watching the young birds they suddenly began to manifest every sign of terror and confusion, chattering and squealing wildly to one another, and flapping their puny wings; and on turning towards the lake in order to ascertain what was the cause of their alarm, he perceived an immense Dragon—whose head, at the end of its long neck, towered high above the water—making its way rapidly across the lake, with the evident intention of devouring the young Gryphons. The young Man, who was of a courageous and kindly disposition, determined to save the young Gryphons from the maw of this monster; so, drawing his sword, he waited till the Dragon had set foot upon dry land, and then, attacking him fiercely, he engaged single-handed in a desperate conflict.

metadata.tibetan_source: Ngo shes (recognition) — the liberating moment of recognizing the wrathful appearances as one's own mind. The key instruction of the Bardo Thodol.
metadata.archetype: The turning point — the hero's moment of facing the dragon (parallels every hero's journey, the pivotal scene in every quest)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Harry Potter facing the Boggart: 'Riddikulus!' The nightmare can only survive if you fear it. Face it, laugh at it, and it transforms into something ridiculous

items.name: Look at Your Hands (The Dreamer's Trick)

sections.Story:
Here's a TRICK. The oldest trick in dreaming.
Before you sleep tonight, say THIS:
'In my dream, I will look at my HANDS.
I will look at my HANDS. I won't miss.'

Say it three TIMES. Mean it. BELIEVE it.
And somewhere tonight, in the MIDDLE
Of a dream about flying or SCHOOL or a dog,
You'll remember — like solving a RIDDLE.

You'll look at your HANDS. And they'll look STRANGE.
Too many fingers. Or GLOWING. Or small.
And you'll think: 'Wait... this is a DREAM.'
And THAT is the most amazing moment of all.
sections.Wonder: Tonight, before you fall asleep, tell yourself three times: 'I will look at my hands in my dream.' If you remember to do it, you might become LUCID — you'll realize you're dreaming while you're still in the dream! What would you do first if you knew you were dreaming?
sections.Whisper: The 'hand check' is from Carlos Castaneda's account of Yaqui dreaming practices, later adopted by Stephen LaBerge in his laboratory research on lucid dreaming at Stanford. In Tibetan dream yoga, the technique is called 'reality testing' (gnas lugs brtag pa) — checking whether your current experience is dream or waking. The Tibetan version goes deeper: check your hands in waking life TOO, because the Tibetans consider waking life ALSO to be a kind of dream. The hand check is not just a dreaming technique — it's a philosophy.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

And so saying he produced a plate from his wallet, and laid upon it the various viands which had been cooked for the Emperor’s dinner the previous day.

When the Emperor understood that this was the dinner which had been prepared for him, and which he ought to have eaten, he was greatly astonished; but seeing that it was no fault of his Cooks, Chamberlains, or other servants, he ordered them all to be released, and to resume their former functions.

Having issued these various commands, the Emperor again summoned the three Thieves before him, and addressed them as follows:

“Although,” said he, “I am very pleased at finding such a satisfactory explanation for the disappearance of the eggs, the misdemeanour of my Prime Minister, and the insufficiency of my dinner, I cannot overlook the fact that you three men have behaved in a very unusual manner. So before rewarding you in accordance with my promise, I desire to put your skill to a further test.

metadata.tibetan_source: Dream yoga reality testing (gnas lugs brtag pa) — checking the nature of experience. Also Carlos Castaneda's 'finding your hands' practice.
metadata.archetype: The reality test — the moment of questioning what is real (parallels 'Am I dreaming?', Descartes' doubt, the red pill/blue pill)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Inception's spinning top — a small object that tells you whether you're in a dream or reality. Except you don't need a totem. You have your hands

items.name: Can You Fly? Then You're Dreaming

sections.Story:
Here's ANOTHER way to know you're dreaming:
Try to FLY. Just jump and... don't come DOWN.
If you float? If the ground gets SMALLER?
Congratulations — you're in DREAMTOWN.

In waking life, you ALWAYS come back down.
Gravity is STRICT. It never takes a BREAK.
But in dreams? Gravity is just a SUGGESTION.
And breaking it is all it takes to WAKE

Up INSIDE the dream. To know you're DREAMING
While the dream is still going ON.
That's lucid dreaming. And once you KNOW?
You're the author. You write the song.
sections.Wonder: In real life, gravity always pulls you down. But in dreams, you can float! If you ever find yourself floating or flying, that's your CLUE that you're dreaming. What would you do with a whole dream you could CONTROL?
sections.Whisper: The 'flight test' is a secondary reality check in lucid dreaming practice — if you suspect you're dreaming, try to defy physics. In Tibetan dream yoga, the ability to fly, pass through walls, or change size are called 'signs of accomplishment' (rtags) in dream practice. But the Tibetan teachers add a crucial caveat: the POINT of lucid dreaming is not to have fun (though fun is fine). The point is to recognize the dream-like nature of ALL experience. If you can change the dream, what else can you change? If gravity is negotiable in dreams, how fixed is it when you're awake?
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

But patience, and the growth of kindly feelings on both sides, helped me to some extent to overcome the shyness and reluctance of the simple folk who have supplied me with my material; and, as time went on, I was able to coax a story from many unlikely sources. Village headmen, monks, servants, local government officials, peasants, traders—these and many others have contributed to my store. Shyly and haltingly at starting, with many bashful apologies and disclaimers, the story-teller will begin his tale. But a Tibetan audience is one of the best imaginable, and their open sympathy and appreciation soon melt the frosts of reserve, and the words flow freely. Presently all sense of constraint is lost, and I have known a story interrupted for ten minutes at a time by the uncontrollable merriment aroused by some comic incident.

Some of the stories, then, I have been obliged, reluctantly enough, to discard altogether for the present; others require further revision or elucidation. But the rest of my little store I give here, and with this one apology: that I have made no attempt to ornament or improve upon them.

metadata.tibetan_source: Dream yoga signs of accomplishment — the ability to transform dream content as evidence of practice maturity.
metadata.archetype: The physics test — checking the rules of reality to determine if you're dreaming (parallels Neo's 'there is no spoon' in The Matrix)
metadata.pop_culture: Like Neo realizing he can dodge bullets — 'Are you telling me I can dodge bullets?' 'No, Neo. I'm telling you that when you're ready, you won't have to.' In dreams, you're ALWAYS ready

sections.Story:
Once you KNOW you're dreaming,
Something magical BEGINS.
You can change the WEATHER with a thought.
You can grow wings. You can grow FINS.

You can make it RAIN candy.
You can turn a monster into a MOUSE.
You can make the ocean CHOCOLATE.
You can build an upside-down HOUSE.

Because the dream is made of YOUR mind.
Every rock, every tree, every CLOUD
Is made of the same stuff as your THOUGHTS.
And thoughts can be QUIET. Or they can be LOUD.
sections.Wonder: If you realized you were dreaming and could change ANYTHING, what would you change first? Would you change the weather? Make animals talk? Visit another planet? The dream is made of your thoughts — you're the architect!
sections.Whisper: Dream control (milam las rnam par rtog pa) is a specific practice in Tibetan dream yoga — the practitioner deliberately transforms dream objects to demonstrate that they are mind-made. Transform fire into water. Transform a mountain into a flower. The POINT is not the transformation itself — it is the RECOGNITION that all appearances are empty of inherent existence (shunyata). If you can turn a mountain into a flower in a dream, it's because neither the mountain nor the flower was ever 'real' — they were always projections of mind. This is the Buddhist teaching on emptiness, experienced directly.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

Once upon a time there was a Cat who lived in a large farm-house in which there was a great number of Mice. For many years the Cat found no difficulty in catching as many Mice as she wanted to eat, and she lived a very peaceful and pleasant life. But as time passed on she found that she was growing old and infirm, and that it was becoming more and more difficult for her to catch the same number of Mice as before; so after thinking very carefully what was the best thing to do, she one day called all the Mice together, and after promising not to touch them, she addressed them as follows:

“Oh! Mice,” said she, “I have called you together in order to say something to you. The fact is that I have led a very wicked life, and now, in my old age, I repent of having caused you all so much inconvenience and annoyance. So I am going for the future to turn over a new leaf. It is my intention now to give myself up entirely to religious contemplation and no longer to molest you, so henceforth you are at liberty to run about as freely as you will without fear of me.

metadata.tibetan_source: Dream transformation practice (milam las) — deliberately altering dream content to recognize the mind-made nature of appearances.
metadata.archetype: The dreamer as creator — the one who shapes reality with thought (parallels the Demiurge, the imagination of God, the Matrix's architect)
metadata.pop_culture: Like being in creative mode in Minecraft — you can build anything, fly anywhere, and nothing can hurt you. Lucid dreaming is the original creative mode

sections.Story:
Here's something WEIRD you can do in a lucid dream:
ASK the dream a question. OUT LOUD.
'Dream! Show me something IMPORTANT!'
'Dream! What do I need to KNOW?' Say it proud.

And the dream ANSWERS. Not in words —
In PICTURES. The whole scene might SHIFT.
A door appears. A path opens UP.
The dream gives you a GIFT.

Because the dream is the WISEST part of you.
The part that knows things you've FORGOTTEN.
The part that sees things you can't SEE.
Ask it anything. Its answers are never ROTTEN.
sections.Wonder: Next time you realize you're dreaming, try this: look up at the dream sky and say, 'Show me something important!' See what happens. The dream might change completely — it might show you something you never expected. What would YOU ask your dream?
sections.Whisper: Asking questions of the dream is a practice found in both Tibetan dream yoga (where the practitioner asks to be shown the nature of mind) and in Western lucid dreaming research (where LaBerge documented dreamers receiving responses from the 'dream environment'). The responses are often surprising, creative, and meaningful — suggesting that the dreaming mind has access to integrative processes the waking mind does not. Jung called this the 'transcendent function' — the capacity of the unconscious to produce solutions the conscious mind cannot reach. The dream is smarter than you think.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

During two years spent in Tibet, at Gyantse, Lhasa, and elsewhere, I have made many friends amongst all classes of Tibetans—high and low, rich and poor—and have conversed with all sorts of persons upon all sorts of topics. In the course of my wanderings I learned that there exists amongst this fascinating and little-known people a wealth of folk-lore, hitherto inaccessible to the outside world, and I made efforts to collect as many of their stories as I could.

For certain special reasons this quest proved more difficult than I had anticipated. In the first place, I found that many of the best known stories had been imported bodily from India [1] or China, and possess but little of that local colouring which is one of the chief charms of folk-lore. Secondly, some of the very best and most characteristic stories are unfit for publication in such a book as this. [2] And, thirdly, human nature being much the same all the world over, it was not always possible to find a suitable raconteur in a suitable mood for story-telling. A story told by a nervous or reluctant narrator loses half its charm.

metadata.tibetan_source: Dream yoga inquiry practice — asking the dream to reveal the nature of mind (sems kyi rang bzhin).
metadata.archetype: The oracle — the wise source that answers questions (parallels the Delphic Oracle, the Magic 8-Ball, the Mirror of Erised)
metadata.pop_culture: Like asking the Sorting Hat to put you somewhere specific — except the dream is way smarter than the Sorting Hat, and its answers are always EXACTLY what you need

items.name: The One Who Watches the Dream

sections.Story:
Here's the DEEPEST question of all:
If you can WATCH the dream,
Who is the WATCHER? Who is the one
Looking at the stream?

You're IN the dream, but also ABOVE it.
You're the actor AND the audience.
You're the movie AND the person watching.
The dreamer and the EVIDENCE.

That watcher? The Tibetans call it RIGPA.
Pure awareness. The SKY behind the weather.
It doesn't change. It doesn't SLEEP.
It's the one thing that holds EVERYTHING together.
sections.Wonder: If you're watching the dream, who is doing the WATCHING? There's a 'you' behind the dream — a quiet, peaceful awareness that just watches. It doesn't sleep. It doesn't get scared. It just watches. Can you feel it right now? The quiet part of you that's just... aware?
sections.Whisper: Rigpa (pure awareness) is the central concept of Dzogchen — the 'Great Perfection,' considered the highest teaching in Tibetan Buddhism. Rigpa is not a thought, not a feeling, not a vision — it is the AWARENESS that is aware of thoughts, feelings, and visions. It is the mirror that reflects everything but is not changed by what it reflects. In dream yoga, the recognition of rigpa WITHIN the dream is considered the highest attainment: you recognize that the dreamer, the dream, and the watching are all expressions of one seamless awareness. This is liberation.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

“This grain is a present in return for your kindness to me. Plant it in your garden and see what grows up.”

The rich man was greatly delighted on hearing this, and thought to himself that he would soon be the possessor of beautiful jewels like his Neighbour. He prepared a bed very carefully in his garden, and planted the grain in the richest part of the soil. Every day he used to go and watch the spot, carefully examining the young shoots to see how they were getting on.

The seeds sprouted and grew very fast, and one morning, when he went out as usual to see how his crop was doing, to his astonishment he found that instead of a few stalks of barley, as he had expected, a great fierce-looking man, with a bundle of papers under his arm, was standing in the middle of the bed. The rich man was very frightened at seeing this truculent-looking stranger, and asked who he was.

“I was a creditor of yours in one of your former existences,” replied the Apparition.

metadata.tibetan_source: Rigpa — pure awareness in the Dzogchen tradition. The recognition of rigpa is the central practice of the Great Perfection.
metadata.archetype: The witness — the consciousness that observes without participating (parallels the Hindu Sakshi, the Taoist observer, Kierkegaard's 'subjective thinker')
metadata.pop_culture: Like the moment in Inception when Cobb realizes he's dreaming within a dream within a dream — except rigpa is the one who's ALWAYS been awake, at every level, watching the whole thing

sections.Story:
Once you know you're DREAMING,
Try something beautiful. Try being KIND.
Find someone in the dream who's SAD
And help them. Heal their MIND.

Give a dream-person a HUG.
Share your dream-food with a STRANGER.
Help a dream-animal that's HURT.
Protect someone from DANGER.

Because KINDNESS doesn't stop being real
Just because you're ASLEEP.
The feelings are real. The PRACTICE is real.
And the habits you build in dreams — you KEEP.
sections.Wonder: Even in dreams, being kind is real. If you help someone in a dream, the feeling of kindness stays with you when you wake up. Your brain can't tell the difference between dream-kindness and awake-kindness! Who would you help in YOUR dreams tonight?
sections.Whisper: Tonglen ('sending and taking') in the dream state is an advanced Tibetan practice: while lucid dreaming, the practitioner breathes in the suffering of dream beings and breathes out compassion. This sounds simple, but it is REVOLUTIONARY: if dream beings are projections of your own mind, then being kind to them is being kind to YOURSELF. All the rejected, frightened, angry parts of you that appear as dream characters — they need your compassion. Dream tonglen is self-compassion practiced at the deepest level. And neuroscience confirms: compassion meditation during dreams activates the same brain regions as waking compassion practice.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

Next morning at daybreak, a great outcry arose from the enemy’s camp. Each man as he rose from his sleep found himself in a woeful plight—his clothes in rags, his pigtail cut off, his bow without a string, his rifle without a sling, and with no fuse or slow-match to fire it, and no provisions for breakfast. Each one began to accuse the other of theft and treachery, and before many minutes had passed the whole camp was in a state of wild confusion, comrade quarrelling with comrade, or accusing their officers of dishonesty and ill-faith.

In the midst of this uproar the sound of bugles was heard on the opposite bank, and a few shots were fired; and terrified at the thought of being taken unawares, the whole army took to flight, and in a few minutes not a man was to be seen.

When the King of the country of the Mice saw what had happened he was greatly elated, and, sending for the King of the Mice, he thanked him very sincerely for his good offices.

metadata.tibetan_source: Dream tonglen practice — sending and taking (compassion meditation) performed within the lucid dream state.
metadata.archetype: The compassionate dreamer — the one who brings kindness into the dream world (parallels the bodhisattva vow, the concept of healing through dreams)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the moment in Inside Out when Joy realizes that Sadness is the one who actually HELPS people. Being kind in dreams is like giving your own sadness a hug — because every dream character IS you

items.name: The Place Where There Are No Dreams

sections.Story:
Between the dreams, there's a PLACE.
No pictures. No stories. No SOUND.
Just... nothing. Not even DARK.
Not even the feeling of the GROUND.

You go there every NIGHT.
You just don't REMEMBER it.
It's the deepest part of SLEEP —
Below the dreams, below the WIT,

Below EVERYTHING. A place so quiet
That even silence is too LOUD.
The Tibetans say this is the MOST important place.
The ground beneath the CLOUD.
sections.Wonder: Every night, you spend time in a place with NO dreams at all — just pure, deep sleep. You can't remember it because there's nothing TO remember. But what if that 'nothing' is actually the most peaceful place in the entire universe?
sections.Whisper: Deep dreamless sleep (sushupti in Sanskrit, nyal ba in Tibetan) is considered by the Tibetan yogis to be the CLOSEST we come to the dharmakāya — the ultimate nature of reality — every single night. In dreamless sleep, the mind rests in its natural state without creating projections. Most people lose consciousness here, but advanced practitioners maintain AWARENESS through dreamless sleep. This is called 'sleep yoga' (nidra yoga), and it is considered more advanced than dream yoga. The goal: to be fully conscious in the formless void. To be awake in the place where there is nothing to be awake TO.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

The Crow was somewhat alarmed at hearing this, and she thought that, on the whole, it would be safer to leave the roof altogether; so again picking up the Frog in her bill she flew off to the ground below, and alighted near the edge of a well. Here she placed the Frog upon the ground and was just about to eat him when the Frog said:

“Oh, Sister Crow, I notice your bill seems rather blunt. Before you begin to eat me don’t you think it would be a good thing to sharpen it a little. You can strop it very nicely on that flat stone over there.”

The Crow, thinking this was a good idea, took two or three hops towards the stone, and began sharpening her bill. As soon as she had turned her back the Frog gave one desperate jump, and dived into the well.

As soon as the Crow had made her bill nice and sharp she returned from the stone, and looked about for the Frog. Not finding him where she had left him she hopped to the edge of the well and peeped over, craning her head from side to side. Presently she spied the Frog in the water, and called out to him:

“Oh, Brother Frog, I was afraid you were lost.

metadata.tibetan_source: Sleep yoga (nidra yoga) — maintaining awareness through dreamless sleep. From the Six Yogas of Naropa and Tenzin Wangyal's dream yoga.
metadata.archetype: The void — the formless ground of being (parallels the Ein Sof, the Tao that cannot be named, Meister Eckhart's Godhead)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the quantum realm in Ant-Man — you go SO deep that the normal rules stop applying. Time doesn't exist. Space doesn't exist. And yet, somehow, it's the source of everything

items.name: The Dark Is Not Empty (It's Full)

sections.Story:
People say 'darkness' like it's BAD.
Like dark means EMPTY, means ALONE.
But the deepest dark is actually FULL —
Full the way a SEED is full of the plant it's GROWN.

Think of the dark inside a SEED.
Inside an EGG. Inside a WOMB.
That dark is not NOTHING — it's EVERYTHING,
Just before it finds its room.

The deep sleep dark is LIKE that.
Full of every dream you'll EVER dream.
Full of every thought you'll ever THINK.
The dark is fuller than it seems.
sections.Wonder: We usually think dark = empty. But think about it: inside a seed, it's dark — and that's where the WHOLE TREE lives! Inside an egg, it's dark — and that's where the bird is growing. What if the darkness of deep sleep is like that? Full of everything, waiting to bloom?
sections.Whisper: Shunyata (emptiness) is the most misunderstood concept in Buddhism. It does NOT mean 'nothing.' It means 'empty of inherent, fixed existence' — which is another way of saying 'full of infinite POTENTIAL.' A cup that is empty can be filled with anything. A mind that is empty of fixed ideas can think anything. The darkness of deep sleep is shunyata experienced directly: the mind rests in its natural emptiness-fullness, free from the projections of dreaming. This is not oblivion. It is the most awake you can be — awake to the ground of being itself.
sections.Original:
— A. L. Shelton, Tibetan Folk Tales (1925) —

The Prince thanked the young lady for all her good advice, which he promised to follow faithfully in every respect, and she then said to him:

“It is now near the time for the Ogre’s return. I will lie down on the couch, and you must place the flower behind my ear just as it was before; and when I fall into a trance you must at once go out into the courtyard and wait the return of the Ogre, and mind you are careful not to let him know that you have been inside the castle.”

So saying, the Princess lay down upon her couch, and the young man having placed the flower behind her ear she instantly fell into a deep trance. The Prince then went out into the courtyard and shortly after the Ogre arrived. He had now discarded his lama costume and appeared in his proper form, and riding up to the Prince he asked him in an angry tone whether he had carried out the orders he had received, and on the Prince replying in the affirmative, the Ogre ordered him to come into the kitchen.

metadata.tibetan_source: Shunyata (stong pa nyid) — emptiness as understood in Madhyamaka and Dzogchen. Emptiness is not nihilism but infinite potential.
metadata.archetype: The pregnant void — the darkness that contains all light (parallels the cosmic egg, the primordial waters, the quantum vacuum)
metadata.pop_culture: Like the inside of WALL-E's hold — it looks like a pile of junk, but it contains everything precious. Or like Mary Poppins' bag — empty-looking but infinite inside

items.name: The Bottom of the Dream Ocean

sections.Story:
If dreams are the WAVES on top,
And the ocean is your MIND,
Then deep sleep is the OCEAN FLOOR —
Dark. Still. The quietest FIND.

No waves down HERE. No currents. No STORMS.
Just the ancient, patient SAND.
And the pressure — not crushing, but HOLDING —
Of a billion tons of water. A soft, firm HAND.

You rest on the ocean FLOOR each night.
The whole ocean ABOVE you, dreaming.
And you? You're at the BOTTOM.
Where everything is silent. And GLEAMING.
sections.Wonder: If your mind is an ocean, then dreams are the waves on top, and deep sleep is the very bottom — the quietest, most peaceful place. What do you think lives at the bottom of YOUR mind-ocean? What treasures might be down there?
sections.Whisper: The ocean metaphor for consciousness is pan-cultural: the Tibetans use it (waves = thoughts, ocean = mind), the Hindus use it (Yoga Sutras compare thoughts to ripples on a lake), and depth psychology uses it (the 'depths' of the unconscious).
